prompt:
Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A software company is losing 5% of users per month (churn). The product
team proposes building 10 new features that exiting users requested
in surveys. The data team notes that users who leave typically used
only 2 of the existing 30 features, while users who stay use an
average of 8 features. What does this suggest the real problem is,
and what would be a more effective intervention than building new
features?

parsed.problem_type: Root cause diagnosis / False solution recognition
parsed.strategy: Data-driven inversion: Compare the characteristics that distinguish successful users from churning users, then recognize what the product team's proposal overlooks
parsed.hardest_part: Resisting the intuitive but incorrect solution (build what users ask for) and trusting the data insight that reveals the actual problem
parsed.solution: The data reveals the real problem is NOT missing features—it's insufficient feature adoption and engagement depth. Churning users have only engaged with ~7% of the product (2/30 features), while retained users have engaged with ~27% (8/30). Building 10 new features for users who haven't unlocked value from 30 existing ones will not solve churn; it will add complexity. The intervention should target feature discovery and activation: (1) Analyze which 8 features drive retention and make them discoverable in onboarding, (2) Improve the activation funnel so new users progress from 2 features to 8+ within their first month, (3) Build guided workflows or feature recommendations that help users discover related features when they're most relevant to their tasks, (4) Measure feature adoption metrics before building anything new. The core issue is engagement with existing features, not feature availability.
